humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates LLMs on multicultural knowledge across 45 countries, covering topics like social etiquette, celebrations, religion, language, and food.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Evaluates text-only QA across two formats: CulturalBench-Easy (multiple-choice) and CulturalBench-Hard (True/False binary judgment of all options).
analytical_tasks_explanation: Factual recognition of regional customs, celebrations, and communication etiquette; reasoning about situations with multiple valid cultural answers (multi-mode behavior).

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Focused specifically on evaluating cultural knowledge and adaptability across 45 global regions, rather than general NLP capabilities.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluated 29 models including GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama-3.3-70b, DeepSeek V3, Qwen-2.5-72b, and OpenAI o1.

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes data related to Language and Linguistics, Religious Studies, History, and Performing Arts (e.g., traditional attire, celebrations, linguistics, and social norms).

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluated using accuracy (specifically multi-choice accuracy for CulturalBench-Easy and joint binary decision accuracy for CulturalBench-Hard).

published_after_march_2023.note: First submitted to arXiv in October 2024 (v2 in June 2025).

english_language.note: The paper is written in English and the benchmark is primarily English-only (with an auxiliary multilingual translation experiment).

summary: CulturalBench is a robust, human-in-the-loop cultural knowledge benchmark consisting of 1,696 questions spanning 45 global regions and 17 cultural topics. Built via Human-AI CulturalTeaming (a collaborative red-teaming pipeline), it features both an Easy (multiple-choice) and a Hard (multi-label binary T/F) evaluation setting to thoroughly assess model understanding of cultural diversity and nuance.
key_findings: While frontier models achieve high accuracy on the Easy multiple-choice version (up to 89.6%), performance drops significantly on the Hard version, where the best model (OpenAI o1) achieves only 61.5% compared to a human baseline of 92.4%. Models systematically struggle with multi-mode questions (multiple valid answers) due to mode-seeking biases, and show uneven geographic performance, excelling in North America/South Asia but underperforming in South America, East Europe, and the Middle East.
